21 (1967) 3855A), attempts to define
ancient
pastoral in the earlier tradition and in the Vergilian
adaptation,
Daphnis becomes a central
heroic figure, incorporating qualities of earlier
literary
and
religious
traditions, a future hero and
hero-lover. The
crisis
behind E.9 and 1 is personal; Octavian's intervention resolved Vergil's dilemma as poet and restored his confidence as man and as artist. Thep¿/erof
E. 4, born into a pastoral context, is fated to reappear in the poet's later
works. Berg deals with relationships between the Eclogues, and with unifying
symbols, and above all, with the pastoral hero, who matures from lovesick
shepherd into the elegiac hero Gallus. But the new hero of E. 4 remained
Vergil's main concern inhis later writing. Berg supplies a revised text and new

Eleanor Leach's volume is concerned with landscape and the relationship
of landscape to the myth of natural freedom. (Both volumes are properly
concerned with the poet's 'place' of inspiration and creation but the arguments and the conclusions differ.) Mrs. Leach rejects the oft-stated interpretation of hostility between the pastoral and the Roman worlds. Theocritan
models notwithstanding, Vergil's poems are invested with a distinctive Roman
atmosphere and symbolism. Cato, Varro, and Vitruvius shed helpful light on
Vergil's portrayals of innocence and landscape, and on his poetic imagination.
Landscape paintings are adduced skilfully and persuasively
to support and
enlarge
the argument. Vergil's pastoral landscapes emerge as symbolic patterns, as poetic compositions which incorporate images of the natural world.
And behind the landscapes resides a philosophy, a personal estimate of man's
condition and of his place inthe environment. Vergil's Corydon and Damon
seek escape into a world of unspoiled innocence; Vergil ismore resolute and
abides with the world of experience, and with the complexity of contemporary civilization.
Pearce's dissertation provides a thematic (or motif) index to the corpus of
ancient bucolic poetry. Mette aligns the puer of E. 4 with the child of Octavia
and Antony, a child with traits of both Antony and Octavian.

Hunting and warfare are analogous to the poet's mind. Crane (10) and
Dunkle (11) concern
themselves with
the motif of the hunt and animal
imagery in their studies of the human conflict, poetic imagery, and conceptions of heroism. Hunt (12) studies the epic design and formal elements that
qualify the poem's theme of glory. The function of gifts, prizes, and trophies,
the gods as superplot, and images of primary importance (land, sea, glory, and
shadow) are major concerns. Perutelli 's critique of Heinze's influential study
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:16:29 UTC
36
of Vergil ¡an epic is sage and
informative. Stachniw's concentrates on the
hero's labores as a key word. Nethercut (15) is concerned with three questions:
the death of Misenus (and of Palinurus) preceding Aeneas' entry into
the Underworld; the departure of Aeneas through the gate of false dreams;
and Turnus' boulder
(A.
12, 896-907) and Homeric counterparts. Vance
deals with the interior states of Vergil's heroes within the framework of the
narrative. Aeneas' righteous anger against Turnus at the close awakens painful, inescapable memories of violence in Rome's past; cultures, like individuals, must learn self-forgiveness to carry on. Williams' book (17) is designed
for schools. Itaccents the ideals of the newly-founded Empire as reflected in
the Aeneid, the problems and advantages of world-destiny, and
offers a
characteristically
sane
and
perceptive appreciation
of the epic
story and
Vergil's poetry. Students are also attuned to Vergil's private voice in the
characterization
and stories of Dido, Turnus, and to Juno's fateful
role.
Wimmel's study of A. 5-8 underscores its Alexandrian nature within Homeric
parentheses.
Pastoral and
rural elements are minor motifs in the ultimate
'quartet' of conflicts. Wimmel's monograph is thought-provoking and highly
instructive. Venus' boots raise several questions which Harrison (19) endeavours to answer: (1) Why did Vergil disguise Venus inthis scene? (2) Why did
he choose the Diana-like huntress for her disguise? (3) Why did he make the
disguise only partial with Venus revealed at the close? and (4) why did he
dress Venus inboots rather than sandals? The answers indicate that the scene
is no sterile episode but one intricately involved with the Dido tragedy. Reed
(20)
argues that because Deiphobe and Aeneas are
real people (not true
umbrae)
they leave the Underworld by the ivory gate which is reserved for
false shades. They
are deceptive (falsae) in the sense that they are living
persons. Controversy continues over the 13 scenes on Aeneas' shield. Eden
argues that Vergil adheres to a perannos, per dies sequence inhis selection of
the first nine scenes to represent 700 years of history. Eden is less concerned
with
artistic examples
than
with chronological progression. The text does
seem
directional: 626, res Italas Romanorum triumphos, and 629, pugnataque in ordine bella. Highet's entertaining article (22) deals with Vergil's
revulsion for sacrificial description (A. 8, 81-85); the white sow and thirty
piglets called
for extended
butchering. Because pork and white wine are
prelude to the passage to Pallanteum, Highet constructs a line to launch their
voyage: ocius ad naves: fugat indigestio somnumi Perutelli and Wiesen shed
fresh
light on
the crucial
eighth book. Wiesen argues that the underlying
colours are dark, although there is no narrative of combat (save Hercules'
encounter
with
Cacus,
and
the
menace of Mezentius)
and no attractive
characters die. West (25) studies the deaths of Hector and Turnus from the
standpoint of organization and content. He argues against the anti-Aeneanism
of Putnam and Quinn on Aeneas' final act and prefers the assessments of
Anderson and Binder. Heroic times and Augustan politics were less concerned
with the morality of a champion's death than with the event itself.
Bryce (26) considers the nature of the Dido-Aeneas relationship and the
underlying tragedy. Two incompatible persons, subject to suspicions, fears,
resentment, despair, and misunderstanding, could only experience a meaning-
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:16:29 UTC
37
less, unproductive relationship. Disaster was the certain outcome. Clawsey's
poem explores the adjustment between Trojan victor and Latin bride. Lewis'
article (28) deals with Aeneas' dual nature, passive impotence and frenetic
activity. 'His vision of the rape of Priam's castle isan encoding of an obsessive
infantile fear:, the threat of the father to kill or annihilate the mother, and by

A. van Rooy, Imitatio of Vergil, Eclogues inHorace, Satires, Book I,'
Acta Classica 16 (1 973) 69-88.
Williams' Presidential address to
the Virgil Society explores reasons for
Vergil's lasting popularity: his variety of poetic mood and style, his ability to
juxtapose different aspects of human behaviour so as to show sympathy for
widely contrasting attitudes of life. Contemporary appreciation realizes the
many-sidedness of the poet's genius and qualities. Segal (43) deals with a
recurrent motif inA. 2, 790-95; 5, 738-40, and 6, 700-702. Earlier usage is
intensified, and there is an evident progression, an artistic maturity, in the
movement from tangible aspects to a more evocative, fugitive event. Luck
(44) restudies Warburton's suggestion (1837) that Aeneas' descent recalls the
procedures, stages, and 'illumination' of initiation into the Eleusinian Mysteries.
Orphic
elements,
detected
by
others,
were
assimilated
into
the
Mysteries and are entirely relevant to the Vergilian descent. Luck's article
brings fresh attention to the richness and complexity of Vergil's art. Oroz
Reta (45) treats political aspects of Roman religion, ritual, cult of the dead,
sacrificial practices, gods and heroes, and Magnus Saeclorum Ordo; a comprehensive,
useful study. Cupaiuolo (47) examines the political and historical
background of Vergil's writings. Van Rooy (48) argues that Horace borrowed
the concept of an organised book of 10 satires, a bipartite division marked by
two introductory poems (1, and 6), and imitated themes and expressions of
(mainly) the corresponding number in the E-book. (Satires 1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 9,
10).

(61) studies similes in A.
12 and adduces Homeric and Callimachean models. George's study of non-Homeric literary tradition inAeneid
8 is provocative and enlightening. He identifies and describes
the aitionsequence of A. 8. The Hellenistic unit provides structural cohesion, a carefully sustained mood from start to finish with variety inintensity and colour,
and a single-tendency
pattern which culminates in the peace of Evander's
angustum tectum. Cassio's article detects Callimachus' Chioma (fr. 110, 1Pf)
inE. 3, 41 (descripsit radio . . . ), A. 6, 850 (describent radio . . . ). A. 6, 851
imitates Cat. 66, 39-40. Glenn (64) detects a Catullian echo (68, 92-3) mA.
3, 658. Schmidt studies Venus' visit to Vulcan (A. 8, 370ff.) as an example of
Vergilian ars et ingenium. Vergil borrows material from Homer, Apollonius
Rhodius, Lucretius, and Catullus to fashion his new construction. Schmidt
counters Macrob. Sat. 1, 24, 7 by rejecting charges of indelicacy inVenus'
request on her son's behalf. Thaniel (66) argues that Vergil (A. 3, 623-6) and
Euripides, Cyclops 396-404, reflect Homer, Od. 288-90.

Eleanor Leach's interpretation of the political Eclogues of Calpurnius Siculus contains useful direction and corrective for critics of Vergil's pastorals.
Hudson-Williams (68) detects imitations of Vergil inValerius Flaccus and a
measure of felicity in the art. Bews (69) shows how Tacitus drew on Vergil
for literary associations. Although the strain of imperial optimism inVergil's
epic is countered by the imperial pessimism of the Annals,Tacitus nevertheless finds inspiration and perspective as literary historian inVergil's Augustan
epic. The Vergilian language which pervades Tacitus' accounts of Tiberius and
Germanicus lends dimension and depth to the historian's interpretation of his
material. Baldwin (70) combs Gellius for critical comments on Vergil's style.

The English poets and T. S. Eliot claim the major attention. Kelsall (73)
examines Trapp's translation of the>Aeneid (1718, 1720) with its extensive
preface and elaborate notes, the best known evaluation of Vergil after Dryden's version. Trapp saw the architecture of the epic as so intricately complex
that its order defied rational explanation. For Trapp, Vergil (and Milton) was
as passionate and as perfect as Bernini and Borromini. Donker (74) indicates
that Eliot evokes the concatenation of events inA. 1-6 inhis Waste Land. He
reformulates this
literary monument of the past as a commentary on the
present and echoes episodes already ingrained inthe literary tradition (Dante,
Spenser, and Milton). Images of The Waste Land have conspicuous analogues
in the Aeneid. Anchises' sermon inA. 6, a recapitulation of Aeneas' own
purgatorial experience as quester, suggests the movement of The Waste Land
as a series of trials by water, wind, and fire. Vergil isboth guide and inspiration for Eliot. Perutelli's study (76) of traditional aspects inEnglish criticism
of Vergil is aligned with Eliot's verdict on the poet. Segal (77) finds Rilke's
version of the Orpheus-Eurydice episode a radical alteration of the original.

The English poets and T. S. Eliot claim the major attention. Kelsall (73)
examines Trapp's translation of the>Aeneid (1718, 1720) with its extensive
preface and elaborate notes, the best known evaluation of Vergil after Dry-
den's version. Trapp saw the architecture of the epic as so intricately complex
that its order defied rational explanation. For Trapp, Vergil (and Milton) was
as passionate and as perfect as Bernini and Borromini. Donker (74) indicates
that Eliot evokes the concatenation of events inA. 1-6 inhis Waste Land. He
reformulates this
literary monument of the past as a commentary on the
present and echoes episodes already ingrained inthe literary tradition (Dante,
Spenser, and Milton). Images of The Waste Land have conspicuous analogues
in the Aeneid. Anchises' sermon inA. 6, a recapitulation of Aeneas' own
purgatorial experience as quester, suggests the movement of The Waste Land
as a series of trials by water, wind, and fire. Vergil isboth guide and inspira-
tion for Eliot. Perutelli's study (76) of traditional aspects inEnglish criticism
of Vergil is aligned with Eliot's verdict on the poet. Segal (77) finds Rilke's
version of the Orpheus-Eurydice episode a radical alteration of the original.
